title: Essential Libraries: requests & pandas
subtitle: HTTP and data wrangling, the Python way

sections.content: Unlike the previous modules, requests and pandas are third-party packages — install them into your venv (Module 10): pip install requests pandas. They're so ubiquitous in Python data/automation work that they're effectively part of the toolkit.

sections.java: // java.net.http HttpClient HttpClient client = HttpClient.newHttpClient(); HttpRequest req = HttpRequest.newBuilder() .uri(URI.create(url)) .header("Authorization", token) .build(); HttpResponse<String> res = client.send( req, BodyHandlers.ofString()); // then parse JSON with Jackson...
sections.python: # requests: one call, JSON built in import requests res = requests.get( url, headers={"Authorization": token}, timeout=10, ) res.raise_for_status() # throw on 4xx/5xx data = res.json() # parsed dict, no Jackson

sections.title: Always Pass a timeout
sections.content: requests has no default timeout — a call can hang forever if the server stalls, exactly the kind of bug that takes down a worker. Always pass timeout=. Use a requests.Session() for connection pooling across many calls (the HttpClient-reuse equivalent), and call raise_for_status() to turn error responses into exceptions. For async workloads, httpx offers the same API with await.

sections.java: // CSV → POJOs → stream aggregation List<Sale> sales = readCsv(path); Map<String, Double> byRegion = sales.stream() .filter(s -> s.amount > 0) .collect(Collectors.groupingBy( Sale::region, Collectors.summingDouble(Sale::amount)));
sections.python: # pandas: vectorized, no POJOs, no loops import pandas as pd df = pd.read_csv(path) by_region = ( df[df.amount > 0] .groupby("region")["amount"] .sum() )

sections.title: pandas Is the Data-Engineering Workhorse
sections.content: A DataFrame is a typed, columnar table — think a List<POJO> plus the entire Streams API, but vectorized in C for speed. read_csv/read_parquet/read_sql load data, then filter, groupby, merge (joins), and pivot replace hand-written stream pipelines. This is the single biggest reason Java teams adopt Python for analytics and ML feature work.

sections.question: What's a critical argument to pass on every requests call in production?
options: timeout — requests has no default and can hang forever otherwise
sections.explanation: requests blocks indefinitely by default if a server never responds, so always pass a timeout. (verify=False disables TLS verification — the opposite of what you want.) Pair it with raise_for_status() and a reusable Session for robust, efficient HTTP.
